nativeRange.habitat: Tropical reef flats, reef slopes, and rubble zones. During the day it tucks under rocks, into crevices, and inside coral rubble; at night it emerges to forage across the substrate and over live rock. It is commonly encountered under rocks among live or dead coral, from the intertidal zone down to about 27 m.

decorPreferences: an established, mature reef tank with plenty of live rock to shelter under during the day
deep crevices, overhangs, and caves so the star can wedge itself completely out of sight
a sand or sand-and-rubble bed for foraging and occasional burying
dim or shaded refuges; this species is strongly nocturnal and avoids bright daytime light

diet.notes: Opportunistic scavenger and detritivore. Sources describe it consuming detritus, uneaten meaty foods, microfauna, and small invertebrate prey it encounters at night. In captivity it has been reported to accept Mysis shrimp, frozen brine shrimp, krill, finely chopped clam or fish, sinking carnivore pellets, and liquid invertebrate foods. Targeted feeding (a small piece placed near the star's daytime refuge at lights-out) is generally advised in well-stocked tanks where competition is high.

goodWith: peaceful medium and large reef fish that ignore brittle stars
established LPS, SPS, and soft corals (no documented coral predation)
reef-safe snails and most cleanup crew invertebrates
larger ornamental shrimp that can keep their distance
avoidWith: very small fish such as gobies or sleeping wrasses that could be ambushed at night
small ornamental shrimp and tiny crustaceans that may be taken when the star is underfed
hermit crabs in undersized tanks (reports of bidirectional aggression and arm-nipping)
wrasses, triggerfish, large hawkfish, pufferfish, and other known echinoderm predators
any system that has been treated with copper-based medications

imageCandidates.notes: Philippe Bourjon, 1200x893. Sharp in-situ reef shot; dark central disc and bristly arms with the diagnostic reddish-brown spine tips, vivid live coloration.
Anne Hoggett (Lizard Island), 800x600. Crisp top-down underwater view of the whole star, black disc with red-highlighted spiny arms; good full-body composition.
Anne Hoggett (High Rock), 800x600. In-situ full animal over rubble, arms clearly showing orange-red spines; different individual/angle for variety.

summary: The Red Brittle Star (Ophiocoma erinaceus) is a nocturnal scavenger from Indo-Pacific reefs, sold under the names Red or Black Brittle Star depending on color phase. It hides in rockwork by day and emerges after dark to forage on detritus and uneaten food.
careNotes:
The Red Brittle Star is the bristly-armed Indo-Pacific brittle star most commonly traded under that name, though the same animal is often sold as 'Black Brittle Star' depending on the color phase of the individual specimen — wild animals range from uniform dark brown or black with red tube feet to specimens with distinctly reddish arm spines. It is distinct from the smoother-armed Ophiolepis serpent stars (which are also generally reef-safe but look quite different) and especially from the predatory Green Brittle Star (Ophiarachna incrassata), which actively hunts small fish and should NOT be confused with this species.

The Red Brittle Star is generally considered reef-safe: no attacks on healthy corals have been documented, and it spends nearly all its active time scavenging detritus and uneaten food across the rockwork at night.

Like all echinoderms, it is extremely sensitive to abrupt changes in salinity, pH, and oxygen, and is intolerant of copper-based medications. Drip acclimation over 2–3 hours is the standard recommendation when introducing a new specimen. A tank age of at least 4–6 months is generally advised so that a stable base of detritivore food — biofilm, microfauna, and accumulated detritus — has time to establish. Adults can reach 25–30 cm from arm-tip to arm-tip and benefit from a tank with a long footprint and abundant live rock to shelter in and forage across; a minimum of 150 L (90 cm tank length) is a reasonable floor for a healthy, long-term setup.
breedingNotes: The Red Brittle Star is not known to be bred in captivity. Wild reproduction follows the standard brittle star pattern of broadcast spawning, where both sexes release eggs and sperm into the water column. The resulting larvae — known as ophioplutei — are microscopic and drift in open water for weeks before settling. Rearing them in a home reef system is impractical: skimmers and mechanical filtration remove the larvae before they can develop, and there is no established hobby protocol for doing so. Virtually all specimens available in the trade are wild-collected.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied primary source root resolved to the 'Brittle Sea Star, Fancy Red' product page (LiveAquaria lists this trade name under Ophiomastix sp., which is visually and behaviorally indistinguishable from Ophiocoma erinaceus in the trade). Provided: 22.2–25.5°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, max ~10 in (~25 cm), reef-safe, semi-aggressive, nocturnal, copper-sensitive, omnivore.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply
additional.notes: Manifest-supplied secondary; the homepage and learning-center search did not return a species-specific care page for Ophiocoma erinaceus or Red Brittle Star at the time of research. Documented as a failed lookup per the playbook fallback rule; substituted by the additional sources below.

additional.name: Reeflex species page (Ophiocoma erinaceus, Spiny Brittle Star)
additional.notes: Species-direct authoritative entry. Confirmed taxonomy (Ophiocomidae / Ophiacanthida), distribution (Indo-Pacific incl. Bali, Indonesia, Philippines, Red Sea, Hawaii, French Polynesia), depth 0–27 m, max ~30 cm total length, 24–29°C, 100 L minimum, care level easy, diet of brine/Mysis/flake. Synonyms: Ophiocoma similanensis, Ophiocoma tartarea.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk care guide (Ophiocoma brittle star)
additional.notes: Genus-level care guide. Confirmed 23–27°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, max ~20 cm diameter, reef-safe with the caveat to avoid hermit crabs, nocturnal, drip-acclimate over several hours, copper-intolerant; flags the unrelated Ophiarachna incrassata as the dangerous look-alike.

additional.name: Saltwateraquarium.com cleanup-crew species page
additional.notes: Trade-source cross-check. Confirmed 22–25.5°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, carnivore/scavenger, reef-safe and peaceful with tankmates, nocturnal, hardy and forgiving for the genus; describes a 10-gallon floor (used here as the low end of the source spread for the cautious-larger tank decision).

additional.name: Saltwateraquariumblog.com Ophiuroidea care guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked lifespan (3–5 years in captivity, up to ~8 years in the wild), 50 gallon (~190 L) recommended minimum for optimal conditions, opportunistic carnivore diet with raw chopped seafood, strong copper sensitivity warning.
